elements.sent-2411-001: Estimators weighting observed outcomes to form an effect estimate have a long tradition.
elements.sent-2411-002: The corresponding outcome weights are utilized in established procedures, e.g. to check covariate balancing, to characterize target populations, or to detect and manage extreme weights.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper provides a general framework to derive the functional form of such weights.
elements.sent-2411-004: It establishes when and how numerical equivalence between an original estimator representation as moment condition and a unique weighted representation can be obtained.
elements.sent-2411-005: The framework is applied to derive novel outcome weights for the leading cases of double machine learning and generalized random forests, with existing results recovered as special cases.
elements.sent-2411-006: The analysis highlights that implementation choices determine (i) the availability of outcome weights and (ii) their properties.
elements.sent-2411-007: Notably, standard implementations of partially linear regression based estimators - like causal forest - implicitly apply outcome weights that do not sum to (minus) one in the (un)treated group as usually considered desirable.
elements.sent-2411-008: Keywords: Augmented inverse probability weighting, causal forest, causal machine learning, covariate balancing, double machine learning, generalized random forest, partially linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-009: Estimating the effect of treatment $D_i$ on outcome $Y_i$ is a common goal in causal inference.
elements.sent-2411-010: This may involve adjusting for confounding variables and/or exploiting exogenous variation induced by an instrumental variable (IV).
elements.sent-2411-011: A variety of estimators is available for each setting depending on the target parameter and the assumptions imposed for identification \cite<see, e.g.~reviews by>{Imbens2009,Athey2017,Abadie2018EconometricEvaluation,Imbens2024CausalSciences .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Many of these estimators are a ``white box'' in the sense that they document how the sample is processed to obtain an effect estimate.
elements.sent-2411-013: Parametric regressions come with familiar coefficient outputs and other popular estimators have a representation as linear combination of observed outcomes: \begin{equation}\label{eq:general-w} \hat{\tau} = \sum_{i=1}^N \omega_i Y_i = \underbrace{\omega'}_{1 \times N} \underbrace{Y}_{N \times 1}\end{equation} where $\omega_i$ represents the weight assigned to the outcome of observation $i$ in estimating $\hat{\tau}$.
elements.sent-2411-014: Structure \eqref{eq:general-w is most prominent in the literature on propensity score matching/weighting \cite<e.g.>{Imbens2015CausalSciences , balancing estimators \cite<e.g.>{Ben-Michael2021TheInference , and synthetic controls \cite<e.g.>{Abadie2021UsingAspects .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Furthermore, it is discussed for estimators of the local average treatment effect \cite<e.g.>{Imbens1997EstimatingModels,Abadie2003SemiparametricModels,Soczynski2024AbadiesEffect as well as for linear regression \cite<e.g.>{Imbens2015MatchingExamples,Chattopadhyay2023OnInference .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Outcome weights $\omega_i$ have established use cases, such as: (i) covariate balancing checks assessing internal validity in experimental and observational studies \cite<e.g.>{Rosenbaum1984ReducingScore,Rosenbaum1985ConstructingScore , (ii) target population characterization investigating external validity in IV settings \cite<e.g.>{Abadie2003SemiparametricModels , or when using OLS \cite{Chattopadhyay2023OnInference , (iii) extrapolation diagnostics for estimators that could use negative weights \cite{Chattopadhyay2023OnInference (v) finite sample estimator stabilization by normalizing weights \cite<e.g.>{Hajek1971CommentOne , or by trimming extreme weights \cite<e.g.>{Lechner2019PracticalEstimation , (vi) variance estimation \cite<e.g.>[Ch.. 19]{Imbens2015CausalSciences .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In contrast, recent estimators integrating supervised machine learning into the estimation process \cite<see>[for a textbook]{Chernozhukov2024AppliedAI can be considered as ``grey box''.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Their multi-step algorithms are transparent and their theoretical properties are well-understood.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, neither coefficients nor outcome weights are currently available to interrogate how these steps jointly process a concrete sample within a concrete implementation.
elements.sent-2411-020: A key take-away of the analysis below is that at least outcome weights can be available to open the grey box.
elements.sent-2411-021: This paper introduces a simple but general framework to derive and analyze outcome weights of form \eqref{eq:general-w .}
elements.sent-2411-022: We establish conditions for a numerical equivalence between an original estimator representation as moment condition and a unique weighted representation.
elements.sent-2411-023: The framework is applied to derive novel outcome weights for the six leading cases of double machine learning \cite{Chernozhukov2018 and generalized random forest \cite{Athey2017a .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Knowing the closed-form of the outcome weights has the immediate practical benefit that they can be plugged into established routines for classic weighting estimators.
elements.sent-2411-025: For example, covariate balancing can now be assessed for conditional average treatment effects estimated by causal forest.
elements.sent-2411-026: A second benefit is that the framework naturally allows to investigate basic properties of the weights.
elements.sent-2411-027: In particular, it highlights that implementation decisions control whether outcome weights of the treated sum up to one and of the untreated to minus one.
elements.sent-2411-028: Such weights are often considered as intuitive, reasonable and desirable in the literature \cite<e.g.>{Imbens2015CausalSciences,Soczynski2024AbadiesEffect .}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, the analysis reveals that estimators building on partially linear regression do not satisfy this property in their standard implementations.
elements.sent-2411-030: The paper makes several contributions: (i) It introduces the first general framework to derive outcome weights; (ii) Its application to causal machine learning estimators yields novel outcome weights and provides a blueprint for applying the framework to other estimators; (iii) It illustrates how the new closed-form expressions enable established diagnostic tools from the weighting literature to be integrated off-the-shelf into causal machine learning applications; (iv) The theoretical results about conditions ensuring desirable estimator properties inform implementation decisions and complement the high-level conditions provided in asymptotic analyses; (v) The paper provides an additional piece in the continuing effort to blur the line between outcome weighting and outcome regression methods.
elements.sent-2411-031: This paper goes in the opposite direction by showing how estimators involving flexible outcome regression can be expressed as weighting estimators; (vi) The accompanying R package \href{https://github.com/MCKnaus/OutcomeWeights {OutcomeWeights computes the weights presented in the paper for general use \cite{Knaus2024OutcomeWeights .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The presented applications rely on this package and can be replicated in a supplementary \href{https://hub.docker.com/repository/docker/mcknaus/outcome_weights/general {Docker image .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Outcome weights in form of \eqref{eq:general-w are leveraged as common structure of difference, weighting, subclassification, and matching estimators \cite[Ch.. 19.4.]{Smith2005DoesEstimators,Huber2013,Imbens2015CausalSciences .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Similarly, the outcome weights are derived and used for ordinary and weighted least squares based estimators \cite{Kline2011Oaxaca-BlinderEstimator,Imbens2015MatchingExamples,Jakiela2021SimpleEffects,Chattopadhyay2023OnInference,Hazlett2024UnderstandingSolutions , two-stage least squares (TSLS) \cite{Chattopadhyay2021OnInference , and augmented inverse probability weighting implemented with (post-selection) OLS outcome regression \cite{Knaus2021ASkills,Chattopadhyay2023OnInference .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: This paper shows that a broader class of estimators can have this structure with a particular focus on those incorporating flexible outcome regression, while covering the results in the literature as special cases.
elements.sent-2411-036: Other types of weights are prominent in the causal inference literature but distinct from the outcome weights pursued in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-037: First, balancing weights are the result of a tailored optimization problem to achieve covariate balancing of some prespecified form \cite{Graham2012InverseData,Hainmueller2012EntropyStudies,Imai2014CovariateScore,Zubizarreta2015StableData,Zhao2019CovariateFunctions,Kallus2020GeneralizedInference,Armstrong2021Finite-sampleUnconfoundedness,Heiler2022EfficientEffect .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Thus, balancing weights are an explicit part of such balancing estimators.
elements.sent-2411-039: While balancing weights are a special case of outcome weights, this paper focuses on estimators where the outcome weights play no explicit role but are implicit in the common characterization of the estimation procedure.
elements.sent-2411-040: Second, effect weights are central to understanding the estimand targeted by a given estimator.
elements.sent-2411-041: The pursued structures in this literature are variations of $\E[w(X_i)\tau(X_i)]$ where $w(X_i)$ is the weight an estimator assigns to the conditional treatment effect $\tau(X_i)$ in expectation.
elements.sent-2411-042: Effect weights are derived under different identifying and functional form assumptions for OLS \cite{Angrist1998EstimatingApplicants,Angrist1999EmpiricalEconomics, Humphreys2009BoundsProbabilities, Aronow2016DoesEffects, Goldsmith-Pinkham2021ContaminationRegressions, Soczynski2022InterpretingWeights , TSLS \cite{Imbens1994IdentificationEffects, Angrist1995Two-stageIntensity,Heckman2005StructuralEvaluation,Soczynski2020WhenLATEb,Blandhol2022WhenLate , two-way fixed-effects \cite<see>[for overviews] {deChaisemartin2023Two-waySurvey,Roth2023WhatsLiterature , regression discontinuity estimators \cite{Lee2010RegressionEconomics , and panel estimators \cite{Chernozhukov2013AverageModels .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The main difference between outcome weights and effect weights is that the former apply to observed outcomes and numerically reproduce the estimate without further assumptions, while the latter weight inherently unobservable effects and usually reproduce the estimate only in expectation.
elements.sent-2411-044: Both types of weights have their established use cases and are therefore complementary.
elements.sent-2411-045: A small but growing body of literature provides nuance to the common notion that it is preferable for the outcome weights to sum to (minus) one within treatment groups.
elements.sent-2411-046: This paper adds the observation that partially linear regression based estimators usually produce weights that overall sum up to zero but not to (minus) one for (un)treated.
elements.sent-2411-047: Finally, the paper adds to recent works establishing numeric equivalences between different estimator representations \cite{Bruns-Smith2023AugmentedRegression or estimators \cite{Soczynski2023CovariateEffects,Soczynski2024AbadiesEffect for conceptual and/or practical insights.}}
elements.section-2411-002: A general framework to derive outcome weights
elements.sent-2411-048: The estimators under consideration require access to data with $N$ observations indexed by $i = 1,...,N$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The data includes a binary treatment $D_i$, an outcome $Y_i$, covariates $\bm{X_i}$, and an optional binary instrument $Z_i$, all collected in $O_i = (D_i, \bm{X_i ', Y_i, Z_i)'$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The empirical mean of a variable $A_i$ is represented as $\E_N[A_i] = N^{-1}\sum_{i=1 ^N A_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Many results are stated in matrix notation where bold letters describe vectors or matrices of variables.
elements.sent-2411-052: This paper focuses on estimators falling into the class of pseudo-IV estimators (PIVE):.
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that the PIVE representation is neither a unique, nor the most compact representation of an estimator.
elements.sent-2411-054: For example, a representation using a linear score $\mathbb{E _N \left[\psi_i^a - \hat{\tau \psi_i^b \right] = 0$ with $\psi_i^a =\tilde{Y _i \tilde{Z _i$ and $\psi_i^b =\tilde{D _i \tilde{Z _i$ would be equivalent, or vice versa any estimator with a linear score can be written as PIVE with $\tilde{Y _i = \psi_i^a$, $\tilde{D _i = \psi_i^b$, and $\tilde{Z _i = 1$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: However, the PIVE structure is essential for the goal of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-056: In particular, separating the pseudo-outcome from the pseudo-instrument makes the derivation and analysis of the outcome weights tractable.
elements.sent-2411-057: Example (OLS): We use the canonical OLS estimator as running example to illustrate the general results throughout the paper.
elements.sent-2411-058: Consider a linear outcome model $Y_i = \tau D_i + \bm{X_i'\beta + \varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The OLS estimator for $\tau$ can be expressed as PIVE using the residual-on-residual regression representation of the Frisch-Waugh-Lovell Theorem: \begin{equation}\label{eq:mom-ols} \mathbb{E}_N \big[ \{\underbrace{Y_i - \bm{X_i^{'}} \hat{\beta}_{\bm{Y} \sim \bm{X}}}_{=: \tilde{Y}_i^{ols}} - \hat{\tau}^{ols} \underbrace{[D_i - \bm{X_i^{'}} \hat{\beta}_{\bm{D} \sim \bm{X}}]}_{=: \tilde{D}_i^{ols}} \} \underbrace{[D_i - \bm{X_i^{'}} \hat{\beta}_{\bm{D} \sim \bm{X}}]}_{=: \tilde{Z}_i^{ols}} \big] = 0\end{equation} where $\hat{\beta _{\bm{Y \sim \bm{X := \bm{(X'X)^{-1 X'Y}$ and $\hat{\beta _{\bm{D \sim \bm{X := \bm{(X'X)^{-1 X'D}$ such that the pseudo-outcome is the outcome residual, and both pseudo-treatment and -instrument are the treatment residual.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Solving Equation \ref{eq:pive leads to parameter estimate \begin{equation}\label{eq:pive1} \hat{\tau} = \frac{\E_N \left[\tilde{Z}_i \tilde{Y}_i\right]}{\E_N \left[\tilde{Z}_i \tilde{D}_i\right]} = (\bm{\tilde{Z}'\tilde{D}})^{-1}\bm{\tilde{Z}'} \bm{\tilde{Y}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Now assume that the pseudo-outcome vector can be obtained by multiplying a unique $N \times N$ transformation matrix $\bm{T}$ with the outcome vector, i.e.~$\bm{TY = \bm{\tilde{Y }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Then, Equation \ref{eq:pive1 can be written in the form of Equation \ref{eq:general-w \begin{equation}\label{eq:pive2} \hat{\tau} = \underbrace{(\bm{\tilde{Z}'\tilde{D}})^{-1}\bm{\tilde{Z}'T}}_{\bm{\omega'}} \bm{Y} = \bm{\omega' Y}\end{equation} leading to a core result of the paper: \begin{prop (outcome weights of PIVE) The outcome weights of a PIVE in the sense of Definition \ref{def:pive have closed-form \begin{equation}\label{eq:general-pl} \bm{\omega'} = (\bm{\tilde{Z}'\tilde{D}})^{-1}\bm{\tilde{Z}'T}\end{equation} if $\bm{\tilde{Z '\tilde{D \neq 0$ and a unique transformation matrix $\bm{T}$ exists such that $\bm{TY = \bm{\tilde{Y }$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: These steps are illustrated below for a variety of estimators.
elements.sent-2411-066: However, the procedure is general and could be pursued for any other estimator fitting into the PIVE structure.
elements.sent-2411-067: Example (OLS) continued: The solution of the residual-on-residual regression \eqref{eq:mom-ols in form of Equation \ref{eq:general-pl is \begin{equation}\label{eq:ols-exmpl} \hat{\tau}^{ols} = \overbrace{(\underbrace{\bm{\hat{E}'}}_{\bm{\tilde{Z}^{ols'}}}\underbrace{\bm{\hat{E}}}_{\bm{\tilde{D}^{ols}}})^{-1} \underbrace{\bm{\hat{E'}}}_{\bm{\tilde{Z}^{ols'}}} \underbrace{M_X}_{T^{ols}}}^{\omega^{ols'}} Y\end{equation} where we use the projection matrix $\bm{P_X := \bm{X(X'X)^{-1 X'}$ to define the residual maker matrix $\bm{M_X := \bm{I_N - P_X}$, and the treatment residual vector $\bm{\hat{E := \bm{M_X D}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The residual maker matrix is therefore the outcome transformation matrix of OLS and $\bm{\omega ^{ols' = (\bm{\hat{E ' \hat{E )^{-1}\bm{\hat{E ' M_X}$ is the outcome weights vector.\footnote{We deliberately do not use that $\bm{M_X}$ is idempotent for illustration purposes but note that also the identity matrix would by a suitable transformation matrix in \eqref{eq:ols-exmpl .}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-098: The instrumental forest (IF) of \citeA{Athey2017a runs an $\bm{x}$-specific weighted partially linear IV regression \begin{equation}\label{eq:mom-cf} \mathbb{E}_N \big[ \{\underbrace{Y_i - \hat{Y}_i}_{=: \tilde{Y}_i^{if}} - \hat{\tau}^{if}(\bm{x}) \underbrace{[D_i - \hat{D}_i]}_{=: \tilde{D}_i^{if}} \} \underbrace{[Z_i - \hat{Z}_i] \alpha_i^{if}(\bm{x})}_{=:\tilde{Z}_i^{if}} \big] = 0\end{equation} where the $\bm{x}$-specific weights $\alpha^{if (\bm{x )$ are obtained by the tailored splitting criterion described in \citeA{Athey2017a and can be extracted via the get\_forest\_weights() function of their grf R package \cite{Tibshirani2024Grf:Forests .}}}}}

elements.subsubsection-2411-004: Augmented inverse probability weighting and its special cases
elements.sent-2411-114: Augmented inverse probability weighting (AIPW) is developed in a series of papers \cite<e.g.>{Robins1994,Robins1995AnalysisData,Rotnitzky1998SemiparametricNonresponse,Chernozhukov2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: AIPW is a PIVE with empirical moment condition \begin{equation}\label{eq:mom-aipw} \mathbb{E}_N \Bigg[\Big\{ \underbrace{\hat{Y}^d_{1,i} - \hat{Y}^d_{0,i} + \lambda_{1,i}^{ipw} (Y_i - \hat{Y}^d_{1,i}) - \lambda_{0,i}^{ipw}(Y_i - \hat{Y}^d_{0,i})}_{=:\tilde{Y}_i^{aipw}} - \hat{\tau}^{aipw} \underbrace{1}_{=:\tilde{D}_i^{aipw}} \Big\} \underbrace{1}_{=:\tilde{Z}_i^{aipw}} \Bigg] = 0\end{equation} and vector form \begin{equation}\label{eq:aipw-4} \hat{\tau}^{aipw} = (\bm{1_N'}\bm{1_N})^{-1} \bm{1_N'}[ \bm{\hat{Y}^d_1} - \bm{\hat{Y}^d_0} + diag(\bm{\lambda_1^{ipw}}) (\bm{Y} - \bm{\hat{Y}^d_1}) - diag(\bm{\lambda_0^{ipw}}) (\bm{Y} - \bm{\hat{Y}^d_0}) ].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-116: The next step is to provide the transformation matrix.
elements.sent-2411-117: This is possible under Condition \ref{cond:smoothers b that the outcome predictions are obtained by smoothers such that $\bm{S^d_d Y = \bm{\hat{Y ^d_d}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Plugging this into \eqref{eq:aipw-4 and rearranging delivers the transformation matrix \begin{equation}\hat{\tau}^{aipw} = N^{-1} \bm{1_N'} [ \underbrace{ \bm{S^d_1} - \bm{S^d_0} + diag(\bm{\lambda_{1}^{ipw}}) (\bm{I_N} - \bm{S^d_1}) - diag(\bm{\lambda_{0}^{ipw}}) (\bm{I_N} - \bm{S^d_0}) }_{=:\bm{T^{aipw}}} ] \bm{Y}\end{equation} and leads to the following result:\footnote{The AIPW implementation of the grf package uses an alternative moment condition.}}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\hat{\tau}^{aipw} = N^{-1} 1_N' [ \underbrace{ S^d_1 - S^d_0 + diag(\bm{\lambda_{1}^{ipw}}) (I_N - S^d_1) - diag(\bm{\lambda_{0}^{ipw}}) (I_N - S^d_0) }_{=:\bm{T^{aipw}}} ] Y\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-119: It is equivalent to \eqref{eq:mom-aipw in expectation but uses different nuisance parameters and therefore differs numerically.}
elements.sent-2411-120: However, also the outcome weights of this variant can be obtained as shown in \ref{sec:app-grf-aipw .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Table \ref{tab:aipw-paths shows how RA can be obtained by setting all IPW weights to zero.}

elements.sent-2411-123: This estimator has the same structure as the canonical \citeA{Wald1940TheError estimator but applies AIPW to estimate reduced form and first stage, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Following \citeA{Chernozhukov2018 , the Wald-AIPW empirical moment condition in the form of Equation \ref{eq:pive reads \begin{equation}\label{eq:iv-ident} E_N \Bigg[ \Bigg\{ \overbrace{ \hat{Y}^z_{1,i} - \hat{Y}^z_{0,i} + \lambda_{1,i}^{ipw,z} (Y_i - \hat{Y}^z_{1,i}) - \lambda_{0,i}^{ipw,z} (Y - \hat{Y}^z_{0,i}) }^{ \tilde{Y}_i^{iv-aipw}:=} \quad - \hat{\tau}^{iv-aipw} \Bigg( \underbrace{\hat{D}_{1,i}^z - \hat{D}_{0,i}^z + \lambda_{1,i}^{ipw,z} (D_i - \hat{D}_{1,i}^z) - \lambda_{1,i}^{ipw,z} (D_i - \hat{D}_{0,i}^z)}_{=: \tilde{D}_i^{iv-aipw}} \Bigg) \Bigg\} \underbrace{1}_{=:\tilde{Z}_i^{iv-aipw}} \Bigg] = 0. \nonumber\end{equation} and in the form of Equation \ref{eq:pive1 becomes \begin{equation}\hat{\tau}^{iv-aipw} = (1_N'[ \bm{\hat{D}^z_1} - \bm{\hat{D}^z_0} + diag(\bm{\lambda_1^{ipw,z}}) (D - \bm{\hat{D}^z_1}) - diag(\bm{\lambda_0^{ipw,z}}) (D - \bm{\hat{D}^z_0}) ])^{-1} \quad \times 1_N'[ \bm{\hat{Y}^z_1} - \bm{\hat{Y}^z_0} + diag(\bm{\lambda_1^{ipw,z}}) (Y - \bm{\hat{Y}^z_1}) - diag(\bm{\lambda_0^{ipw,z}}) (Y - \bm{\hat{Y}^z_0}) ]. \nonumber\end{equation} Following similar steps as in Section \ref{sec:aipw-ow establishes another special case of Proposition \ref{prop:iw-pive :.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-129: However, it is important to note that the choices for instrument and treatment nuisance parameters do not affect the availability of outcome weights.
elements.sent-2411-130: Overall, the simple framework of Section \ref{sec:general-framework proves very handy for compactly deriving new functional forms of outcome weights and recovering known ones.}
elements.sent-2411-131: This is interesting and practically useful in its own right, as the obtained weights can be applied in any established weight-based routine.

elements.sent-2411-133: The results of the previous section enable users to ex post inspect whether outcome weights fulfill certain properties.
elements.sent-2411-134: For example, weights adding up to one for the treated (i.e. $\sum_i \omega_i D_i = 1$) and to minus one for the untreated (i.e. $\sum_i \omega_i (1-D_i) = -1$) are often considered desirable because they guarantee certain in- and equivariances of estimators \cite{Imbens2015CausalSciences, Soczynski2024AbadiesEffect .}
elements.sent-2411-135: However, the PIVE framework also allows to analytically investigate the weights properties of estimator implementations.
elements.sent-2411-136: This is conceptually appealing and practically relevant because it permits ex ante control over weights properties.

elements.sent-2411-222: They contain several surprising or at least undocumented results regarding the six DML and GRF leading cases:.
elements.sent-2411-223: PLR(-IV), causal/instrumental forests, and Wald-AIPW are not fully-normalized in standard implementations because they usually apply different treatment and outcome models (C\ref{cond:osmatt not fulfilled).}
elements.sent-2411-224: AIPW is fully-normalized in standard implementations because they usually apply affine smoothers and estimate treated and untreated outcomes separately (C\ref{cond:affine-smooth \& C\ref{cond:nsbg fulfilled).}}
elements.sent-2411-225: This section runs an Empirical Monte Carlo Study (EMCS) to illustrate that most standard implementations of DML and GRF are not fully-normalized.
elements.sent-2411-226: EMCS take a real dataset and modify some components such that the ground truth is known in the semi-synthetic dataset \cite{Huber2013,Wendling2018,Knaus2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-227: Here, we use the treatment, instrument, and covariates of the 401(k) data \cite{Chernozhukov2016High-DimensionalR but with a noiseless outcome $Y_i^* = 1 + D_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-228: This simulates the most powerful treatment leaving every untreated unit at one and shifting every treated unit to two.
elements.sent-2411-229: We expect estimators to estimate an effect of exactly one in this setting without outcome noise.
elements.sent-2411-230: However, only fully-normalized estimators are guaranteed to achieve this because for them, $\bm{\omega' Y^* = \bm{\omega' (\bm{1_N + \bm{D ) = 1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-231: This exercise is run with the DoubleML \cite{Bach2024DoubleML:R and the grf \cite{Tibshirani2024Grf:Forests R packages applied to 100 bootstrap samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: The nuisance parameters in DoubleML are obtained using honest random forest (affine smoother) or XGBoost (non-affine smoother).
elements.sent-2411-233: Each function uses its default values.
elements.sent-2411-234: Table \ref{tab:emcs-estimators summarizes the ten implementations under consideration.}
elements.sent-2411-235: The final column shows whether an implementation is fully-normalized according to the theoretical results in Table \ref{tab:iw-props and therefore expected to find the ``effect'' of one.}

elements.sent-2411-245: First, we investigate covariate balancing for DML estimated average effects.
elements.sent-2411-246: PLR(-IV) and (Wald-)AIPW are implemented using honest random forests with 2- and 5-fold cross-fitting.
elements.sent-2411-247: Figure \ref{fig:balance-401k presents canonical balancing plots from the cobalt R package \cite{Greifer2024Cobalt:Plots displaying absolute standardized mean differences (SMD).}}
elements.sent-2411-248: We observe that each method successfully balances the previously unbalanced covariates, in particular the income variable.
elements.sent-2411-249: Furthermore, cross-fitting with 5-folds achieves better covariate balancing compared to 2-folds.
elements.sent-2411-250: This demonstrates how DML outcome weights can be utilized in the design phase described by \citeA{Rubin2007TheTrials , allowing researchers to commit to the preferred implementation before examining the results.}

elements.sent-2411-256: The results show that tuning the forests substantially improves covariate balancing in this application.

elements.sent-2411-265: More estimators than previously noted can be expressed as weighted outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-266: The paper provides a general framework and derives novel weights for double machine learning and generalized random forest estimators.
elements.sent-2411-267: A key learning is that both availability and properties of the outcome weights depend on implementation choices and are therefore controlled by the user.
elements.sent-2411-268: The paper focuses on providing general theoretical tools and standard illustrations.
elements.sent-2411-269: This acknowledges that access to their closed-form expressions is a prerequisite for developing new use cases or theoretical results for outcome weights.
elements.sent-2411-270: With the provided tools now available, many follow-up questions arise for future research:.
elements.sent-2411-271: Are there estimator specific use cases beyond the standard diagnostic tools?.
elements.sent-2411-272: What are the closed-form expressions and properties of other PIVE outcome weights?.
elements.sent-2411-273: Does the finding that several popular estimators do not use fully-normalized weights challenge the preference for such weights in the literature, or could explicitly normalizing the weights improve the finite sample performance of these estimators?.
elements.sent-2411-274: Does the need to restrict outcome predictors to smoothers for access to outcome weights suggest a trade-off between interpretability and performance for outcome adaptive causal effect estimators?.
elements.sent-2411-275: Do the provided outcome weights have implications for statistical inference, asymptotic properties, or double robustness robustness properties?.
elements.sent-2411-276: The investigation of the latter point most likely requires to restrict focus to analytically tractable smoothers in contrast to the generic smoothers allowed for in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-277: The fact that the smoothers and therefore the outcome weights may depend on the outcome pose non-trivial challenges.
elements.sent-2411-278: For example, it makes the outcome weights not compatible with approaches to use them for statistical inference as the existing approaches require outcome weights to be independent of the outcomes \cite<e.g.>[Ch.. 19]{Imbens2015CausalSciences .}
elements.sent-2411-279: Tailored sample splits as in \citeA{Lechner2018 could ensure the required independence but explorations along these lines are left for future work.}
elements.sent-2411-280: Let $Y_i(1)$ and $Y_i(0)$ be the potential outcomes under treatment and control, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-281: The paper is motivated by estimators of causal effects that aggregate the individual treatment effects $Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)$ over different populations:.
elements.sent-2411-282: Alternatively, we might impose a partially linear outcome model $Y_i = \theta D_i + g(\bm{X_i ) + \epsilon_i$ and aim to estimate $\theta$.}

elements.sent-2411-285: Table \ref{tab:estimators collects how the considered estimators differ in the aggregation level of the target effect (average or conditional effects), the research design in which they are usually applied (randomized controlled trials, unconfoundedness or instrumental variables), and regarding outcome modelling assumptions (none, partially linear, or linear models).}
elements.sent-2411-286: Many common regression estimators admit a representation as smoother.
elements.sent-2411-287: We distinguish three classes of smoothers:\footnote{See for a similar discussion the recent literature using smoothers to explain properties of machine learning methods \cite{Curth2023ALearning,Curth2024WhySmoothers .}}

elements.sent-2411-321: Estimators that weight observed outcomes to form effect estimates have a long tradition.
elements.sent-2411-322: Their outcome weights are widely used in established procedures, such as checking covariate balance, characterizing target populations, or detecting and managing extreme weights.
elements.sent-2411-323: This paper introduces a general framework for deriving such outcome weights.
elements.sent-2411-324: The framework is applied to derive novel outcome weights for the six seminal instances of double machine learning and generalized random forests, while recovering existing results for other estimators as special cases.
elements.sent-2411-325: Notably, standard implementations of partially linear regression-based estimators, like causal forests, employ outcome weights that do not sum to (minus) one in the (un)treated group, not fulfilling a property often considered desirable.
elements.sent-2411-326: Keywords: Augmented inverse probability weighting, causal forest, causal machine learning, covariate balancing, double machine learning, generalized random forest, implied weights, partially linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-327: A variety of estimators is available for estimating different target parameters, after arguing for their identification within a particular research design \cite<see, e.g.~reviews by>{Imbens2009,Athey2017,Abadie2018EconometricEvaluation,Imbens2024CausalSciences .}
elements.sent-2411-328: Outcome weights $\omega_i$ have established use cases, such as: (i) covariate balancing checks assessing internal validity in experimental and observational studies \cite<e.g.>{Rosenbaum1984ReducingScore,Rosenbaum1985ConstructingScore , (ii) target population characterization investigating external validity in IV settings \cite<e.g.>{Abadie2003SemiparametricModels , or when using OLS \cite{Chattopadhyay2023OnInference , (iii) extrapolation diagnostics for estimators that could use negative weights \cite{Chattopadhyay2023OnInference , (iv) finite sample estimator stabilization by normalizing weights \cite<e.g.>{Hajek1971CommentOne , or by trimming extreme weights \cite<e.g.>{Lechner2019PracticalEstimation , (v) variance estimation \cite<e.g.>[Ch.. 19]{Imbens2015CausalSciences .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-329: A key take-away of the analysis below is that at least outcome weights can be available for such multi-step estimators.
elements.sent-2411-330: The framework is applied to derive novel outcome weights for the six seminal instances of double machine learning \cite{Chernozhukov2018 and generalized random forest \cite{Athey2017a .}}
elements.sent-2411-331: However, the new framework reveals that estimators building on partially linear regression do not satisfy this property in standard implementations.
elements.sent-2411-332: Example (OLS): We use the canonical OLS estimator as a running example to illustrate the general results throughout the paper.
elements.sent-2411-333: This section leverages the new framework to provide the first characterization of outcome weights for six seminal instances within the double machine learning (DML) and generalized random forest (GRF) frameworks (marked with $^*$), while also recovering existing results for eight other estimators:.
